title: When Big Companies Fed the World's Data In
subtitle: The race that built giant AI models
theme: Today is about awe and perspective: feeling the sheer scale of money, machines, and data behind modern AI, while seeing the very human race that made it happen.
learningObjectives: Explain in plain words why training a serious AI needs enormous money and special chips called GPUs
Describe where AI's training data actually comes from: books, the open web, and computer code
Tell the story of the compute race and why a chip company named NVIDIA suddenly became one of the most valuable companies on Earth
Recognize the first 'giant' AI models, BERT and GPT, and what made them feel like a turning point
Understand who could afford to build these giants, and how their arrival quietly changed life for everyone else
storyHook: Yesterday we watched something almost magical happen: feed a network enough examples, and patterns start to look a lot like understanding. But here's the catch nobody tells you at first. To get that magic at full strength, you don't need a clever trick. You need a warehouse full of expensive machines, an electricity bill the size of a small town's, and basically a copy of everything humans have ever written. Today is the story of the few companies who decided to actually do that, and the race that followed.

slides.title: Welcome to the Day Everything Got Huge
slides.body:
Welcome back. So far our story has been about ideas: attention, transformers, turning words into numbers, neural networks learning from examples. Yesterday we saw that when you pour in enough data, a network's behavior starts to look surprisingly intelligent.

Today the story changes gears. It stops being only about clever ideas and starts being about raw scale: money, machines, and mountains of data.

Think of it like cooking. For years, researchers had a great recipe but only a tiny kitchen. Then a handful of companies built industrial kitchens the size of factories, bought every ingredient on the planet, and started cooking nonstop. The recipe was the same. The results were jaw-dropping, simply because everything got bigger.

This matters because it explains the AI world you live in now. Why do a few big names dominate? Why is a chip company suddenly worth trillions? Why did 'AI' go from a research word to something on your phone? It all traces back to this chapter: the moment big companies fed the world's data in.
slides.speakerNotes: Set an excited, slightly awestruck tone. Emphasize the shift from 'ideas' to 'scale' as the day's big theme.
slides.marketConnection: Every chatbot, search summary, and writing assistant you touch in 2026 exists because a few companies decided scale was worth the staggering cost.

slides.title: Why Real Scale Needs Real Money
slides.body:
Let's start with an uncomfortable truth: serious AI is shockingly expensive to build.

Remember that learning, for a neural network, means doing math over and over, adjusting millions or billions of tiny dials until it gets good at predicting. Now imagine doing that math not a few thousand times, but trillions of times, on a dataset the size of the whole internet. That is an unimaginable amount of arithmetic.

Here is an everyday analogy. One person with a calculator could technically add up a country's entire tax records. They'd just need a few thousand years. Useless. So instead you hire a giant building full of people all adding at once. AI does the same thing, but with chips instead of people, and the building is a data center.

That building costs a fortune: the machines, the electricity, the cooling, the engineers. Training one big model can cost tens or hundreds of millions of dollars. This single fact, that scale costs serious money, shapes everything else in today's story.
slides.speakerNotes: Land the core intuition: learning is repeated math, and scale means doing that math an astronomical number of times.
slides.marketConnection: When you hear that a company spent hundreds of millions training a model, this repeated-math-at-massive-scale is exactly where that money goes.

slides.title: The Special Chips Called GPUs
slides.body:
So you need to do a colossal amount of math, fast. The hero of this part of the story is a special kind of chip: the GPU, short for Graphics Processing Unit.

Here's the funny twist. GPUs were originally invented for video games, to draw fast-moving 3D worlds on screen. Drawing graphics means doing a huge number of small, similar calculations all at the same time. It turns out that is exactly what training a neural network needs too.

Think of your computer's main chip, the CPU, as one brilliant chef who can cook any dish but only one at a time. A GPU is more like a thousand line cooks who each do one simple step, all at once. For fancy single dishes, the chef wins. For making ten thousand identical sandwiches, the army of line cooks wins by a mile. AI training is the ten-thousand-sandwiches problem.

That's why companies don't buy one GPU. They buy tens of thousands and wire them together into one enormous brain-building machine.
slides.speakerNotes: The CPU-chef vs GPU-line-cooks analogy is the keeper here. Make sure they grasp 'many small things at once'.
slides.marketConnection: Cloud services like those from Amazon, Microsoft, and Google rent out GPU time by the hour, which is how startups train AI without owning a warehouse of chips.

slides.title: Where Did All the Data Come From?
slides.body:
A giant machine is useless without something to learn from. So where did these companies get enough text to fill a mind? The honest answer: from us. From almost everything we've written down.

Three big sources fed the early giants. First, books, millions of them, giving careful, well-edited language and deep knowledge. Second, the open web, billions of pages: articles, forums, Q&A sites, recipes, arguments, jokes, the messy chatter of humanity. Third, code, the actual programs developers share publicly online, which is how AI learned to write software too.

Imagine raising a child who, instead of reading a few hundred books in a lifetime, somehow read most of the public internet plus a library larger than any on Earth. They wouldn't understand it like a wise elder, but they'd have seen patterns in language no human ever could.

That's the trade. The web is gloriously rich but also full of errors, bias, and nonsense. So a model learns the good and the bad together, which is a theme we'll keep returning to.
slides.speakerNotes: Name the three sources clearly: books, web, code. Plant the seed that the data's flaws become the model's flaws.
slides.marketConnection: Debates in 2026 about AI and copyright, and why news sites and artists are pushing back, come straight from this question of whose data trained these models.

slides.title: The Compute Race and NVIDIA's Explosion
slides.body:
Now picture this as a gold rush. The 'gold' is smarter AI. The 'shovels' are GPUs. And history's lesson about gold rushes is that the people who sell the shovels often get richest of all.

Enter NVIDIA, a company that had quietly spent years making those game-graphics chips. When the AI world realized GPUs were the perfect tool for training, every serious player wanted them, and NVIDIA was the main place to get them. Demand went vertical.

A company most non-gamers had never heard of became one of the most valuable on the planet, worth trillions of dollars. Not because they built the famous chatbots, but because nearly every chatbot was trained on their hardware.

This kicked off a race. Each company tried to grab more chips than its rivals, because more chips meant bigger models meant a bigger lead. It became a contest of who could assemble the most computing power, often just called 'compute'. The phrase you'll hear is simple and a little wild: in this era, more compute often meant more capability.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the gold-rush / sell-the-shovels framing as the emotional center. NVIDIA = the shovel seller.
slides.marketConnection: NVIDIA's stock and the global scramble for its chips were front-page financial news through 2024-2026, and shortages still shape who can build AI today.

slides.title: The First Giants Appear: BERT and GPT
slides.body:
With money, machines, and data in place, the first true giants arrived, and two names led the way: BERT and GPT.

BERT, from Google in 2018, was like a brilliant reader. Its trick was learning to fill in blanks by looking at words on both sides at once, the way you'd guess a missing word in a sentence using the whole sentence for clues. This made search engines and language tools suddenly much better at understanding what we mean.

GPT, from a lab called OpenAI, was more like a brilliant writer. The 'G' stands for 'generative', meaning it produces new text. Its talent was predicting the next word, over and over, until it had written whole sentences and paragraphs that actually made sense. Each version, GPT-2, then GPT-3, got dramatically bigger and more capable.

Think of BERT as the friend who truly understands what you said, and GPT as the friend who can keep talking fluently for hours. Both were proof that the recipe plus massive scale really did produce something new.
slides.speakerNotes: Keep it to two clean characters: BERT the reader (fill-in-the-blank), GPT the writer (next-word). Don't overload with versions.
slides.marketConnection: The 'GPT' in the chatbots millions use daily is this exact lineage; BERT's ideas quietly power a lot of search and language features behind the scenes.

slides.title: Who Could Afford to Play
slides.body:
Here's the catch hiding inside this triumph. If building a giant model needs tens of thousands of GPUs, a data center, and tens of millions of dollars, then a curious genius in a garage simply cannot do it anymore.

For a while, only a small club could play: the biggest tech companies and a few extremely well-funded labs. It's a bit like space travel. Anyone can dream of the moon, but only organizations with enormous budgets can actually launch a rocket. AI's frontier became a place you needed a fortune to even enter.

This concentrated remarkable power in a few hands, which made many people uneasy, and that unease is still very much alive today.

But the story has a hopeful turn. Once a giant model is built, others can study it, copy the approach more cheaply, or release 'open' versions that everyone can use. So the club that builds from scratch stays small, but the circle of people who can use these models keeps getting wider and wider.
slides.speakerNotes: Balance two feelings: concern about concentration of power, and hope that access widened even if creation stayed exclusive.
slides.marketConnection: This is why a handful of companies dominate AI headlines, yet a fast-growing world of open models lets startups and hobbyists build on top without billion-dollar budgets.

slides.title: What Changed for Everyone
slides.body:
Step back and notice how much your everyday world quietly shifted because of this race.

Before these giants, software did exactly, and only, what it was programmed to do. After them, your phone could finish your sentences, your email could draft replies, your search bar could answer in full paragraphs instead of just listing links. None of that required you to spend a fortune. The giants did the expensive part once, and the benefits trickled out to everyone.

Imagine if, for centuries, every village had to invent its own electricity, and then suddenly a few power plants got built and the whole country could just plug in. That's roughly what happened. A few players paid the staggering cost of building the 'power plants' of intelligence, and the rest of us got to plug our little appliances in.

That shift, from AI as a rare research toy to AI as something woven into ordinary tools, is the real payoff of today's story. And it sets up a beautiful question for tomorrow.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the electricity / power-plant analogy to make scale feel like shared infrastructure, not just a corporate flex.
slides.marketConnection: Autocomplete, smart replies, AI search summaries, and writing helpers you use for free in 2026 are all 'plugging in' to giants someone else paid to build.

slides.title: Recap and the Big Picture
slides.body:
Let's gather today's story into a few simple truths.

First, the leap from clever idea to real power came from scale: doing the same learning math an astronomical number of times. That takes serious money.

Second, the engine of that scale is the GPU, the chip that, like an army of line cooks, does countless small calculations at once. To go fast, companies wire tens of thousands together.

Third, the fuel is data, mostly our own books, web pages, and code, feeding the model both humanity's wisdom and its messiness.

Fourth, the scramble for chips made NVIDIA, the shovel seller in this gold rush, one of the most valuable companies in the world.

Fifth, the first giants, BERT the reader and GPT the writer, proved scale truly works.

And finally, only a tiny, wealthy club could build them, but the benefits, and increasingly open versions, spread to all of us.

You now understand the machinery behind modern AI. Tomorrow we ask a deeper question: what exactly are these giant brains, and why can one of them do so many different jobs?
slides.speakerNotes: Recap as six crisp truths. End by pivoting curiosity toward foundation models.
slides.marketConnection: Understanding scale, chips, and data is the literacy that lets you read AI news in 2026 and tell genuine progress from pure hype.

keyTakeaways: Modern AI's biggest leaps came less from new ideas and more from raw scale: huge money, huge machines, and huge data.
GPUs are special chips that do enormous numbers of small calculations at once, like an army of line cooks instead of one master chef.
AI's knowledge comes mostly from us: books, the open web, and public code, which means it learns our wisdom and our flaws together.
NVIDIA became one of the world's most valuable companies by selling the 'shovels' (GPUs) for the AI gold rush.
BERT (a brilliant reader) and GPT (a brilliant writer) were the first giant models proving that scale really produces new capability.
Only a few wealthy companies can build giants from scratch, but the benefits and open versions have spread AI to nearly everyone.
marketSnapshot2026: As of 2026, the AI world is still shaped by this race. NVIDIA remains a multi-trillion-dollar company and its chips are so sought-after that access to them is a competitive advantage in itself. A small group of well-funded labs and big tech firms train the largest models, while a thriving ecosystem of open and smaller models lets startups, schools, and individuals build powerful tools without owning a data center. Meanwhile, the question of whose books, art, and code were used as training data has moved from a niche debate into courtrooms, licensing deals, and national policy.
homework: Pick one AI tool you use or have heard of (a chatbot, an email autocomplete, a search summary). In three or four sentences, guess: What data might it have learned from? Whose 'power plant' is it likely plugging into? Don't worry about being right, just practice seeing the scale, chips, and data hiding behind something that feels effortless.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow, Day 8: 'Foundation Models: One Brain, Many Jobs.' We've built our giant. Now the surprising part: a single trained model can write poems, answer questions, summarize reports, and help with code, all without being separately taught each task. How can one brain be so versatile? That's where we head next.
